instruction: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled, and when should you use each? Show practical examples where choosing wrong causes bugs.
output:
<think>
Promise.all and Promise.allSettled have fundamentally different failure semantics. Promise.all short-circuits on the first rejection — if any promise fails, the entire result is a rejection, and you lose the results of promises that succeeded. Promise.allSettled waits for ALL promises to complete (fulfilled or rejected) and returns an array of result objects with status/value/reason fields. The critical bug case with Promise.all is when you're doing independent operations that shouldn't fail together. For example, fetching data from 5 independent APIs — if one is down, you still want the other 4 results. Using Promise.all means one failure kills everything. Conversely, Promise.all is correct when the operations are interdependent — like a database transaction where partial success is worse than total failure. Another subtle issue: Promise.all with an empty array resolves immediately, which can mask bugs in dynamic arrays where you expected items. Promise.allSettled with an empty array also resolves immediately but forces you to handle the empty case in your result processing.
</think>

```javascript
// === Promise.all: Fail-fast — ONE rejection kills everything ===

async function fetchAllOrNothing(urls) {
  try {
    // If ANY fetch fails, ALL results are lost
    const responses = await Promise.all(
      urls.map(url => fetch(url).then(r => r.json()))
    );
    return responses;
  } catch (err) {
    // You only get the FIRST error — successful results are gone
    console.error('All lost because one failed:', err.message);
    return [];
  }
}

// BUG: API-3 is down, but API-1 and API-2 succeeded
// Promise.all throws away the successful results!
await fetchAllOrNothing([
  'https://api-1.com/data', // ✅ succeeded
  'https://api-2.com/data', // ✅ succeeded  
  'https://api-3.com/data', // ❌ 503 error — kills everything
]);

// === Promise.allSettled: Get ALL results regardless ===

async function fetchBestEffort(urls) {
  const results = await Promise.allSettled(
    urls.map(url =>
      fetch(url)
        .then(r => {
          if (!r.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${r.status}`);
          return r.json();
        })
    )
  );

  const succeeded = results
    .filter(r => r.status === 'fulfilled')
    .map(r => r.value);

  const failed = results
    .filter(r => r.status === 'rejected')
    .map((r, i) => ({ url: urls[i], error: r.reason.message }));

  console.log(`${succeeded.length}/${results.length} succeeded`);
  if (failed.length > 0) {
    console.warn('Failed:', failed);
  }

  return { succeeded, failed };
}

// API-3 fails but we still get API-1 and API-2 data
const { succeeded, failed } = await fetchBestEffort([
  'https://api-1.com/data',
  'https://api-2.com/data',
  'https://api-3.com/data',
]);
// succeeded: [data1, data2], failed: [{url: 'api-3', error: 'HTTP 503'}]

// === When to use Promise.all: interdependent operations ===

async function transferFunds(from, to, amount) {
  // These MUST both succeed — partial transfer = data corruption
  const [debit, credit] = await Promise.all([
    db.query('UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - $1 WHERE id = $2', [amount, from]),
    db.query('UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + $1 WHERE id = $2', [amount, to]),
  ]);
  return { debit, credit };
  // If one fails, Promise.all rejects — you catch and rollback
}

// === Gotcha: Promise.all with empty array ===
const items = []; // Dynamic — might be empty
const results = await Promise.all(items.map(processItem));
// results is [] — resolves immediately, no error
// This can silently do nothing if items is unexpectedly empty
```

instruction: Explain common promise chaining gotchas in JavaScript. What mistakes do developers make when chaining .then() calls?
output:
<think>
Promise chaining has several subtle gotchas that trip up even experienced developers. The biggest one is forgetting to return inside a .then() callback — if you don't return, the next .then() receives undefined instead of your value. This is especially insidious because the chain doesn't error; it just silently passes undefined. Another common mistake is nesting .then() calls instead of chaining them, recreating callback hell with promises. The third gotcha is error handling placement — a .catch() at the end catches everything, but a .catch() in the middle swallows the error and the chain continues with undefined. You need to re-throw if you want downstream catches to fire. Then there's the async/await gotcha where people mix .then() and await inconsistently, creating confusing control flow. Finally, people forget that .then() always returns a NEW promise — mutating a variable in .then() and reading it after doesn't work because .then() is asynchronous.
</think>

```javascript
// GOTCHA 1: Forgetting to return in .then()
// The chain silently passes undefined to the next step

fetch('/api/user')
  .then(response => {
    response.json(); // ❌ Missing return! Result is undefined
  })
  .then(data => {
    console.log(data); // undefined — silent bug, no error thrown
  });

// FIX: Always return (or use arrow without braces)
fetch('/api/user')
  .then(response => response.json()) // ✅ implicit return
  .then(data => {
    return processData(data); // ✅ explicit return
  })
  .then(result => console.log(result));

// GOTCHA 2: Nesting instead of chaining
// Creates pyramid of doom — defeats purpose of promises

// ❌ BAD: Nested
getUser(id).then(user => {
  getOrders(user.id).then(orders => {
    getDetails(orders[0].id).then(details => {
      console.log(details); // Callback hell with promises
    });
  });
});

// ✅ GOOD: Chained (flat)
getUser(id)
  .then(user => getOrders(user.id))
  .then(orders => getDetails(orders[0].id))
  .then(details => console.log(details));

// GOTCHA 3: .catch() swallows errors and chain continues

fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .catch(err => {
    console.error('Failed:', err);
    // ❌ No return/rethrow — next .then() gets undefined
  })
  .then(data => {
    // This runs even after error! data is undefined
    console.log(data.name); // TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
  });

// FIX: Re-throw in catch, or return a recovery value
fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .catch(err => {
    console.error('Failed:', err);
    return { name: 'fallback' }; // ✅ Recovery value
  })
  .then(data => {
    console.log(data.name); // 'fallback' — safe
  });

// GOTCHA 4: Reading variable after .then() — async timing
let result = null;

fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(data => {
    result = data; // Runs later (async)
  });

console.log(result); // ❌ null — .then() hasn't executed yet!

// FIX: Stay inside the chain, or use async/await
async function loadData() {
  const response = await fetch('/api/data');
  const result = await response.json();
  console.log(result); // ✅ Correct order
  return result;
}

// GOTCHA 5: .then(fn) vs .then(fn())
Promise.resolve(42)
  .then(console.log)     // ✅ Passes 42 to console.log
  .then(console.log());  // ❌ Calls console.log() immediately, passes return value (undefined)
```

instruction: How should you handle unhandled promise rejections in Node.js? What changed across Node versions and what are best practices?
output:
<think>
Unhandled promise rejections have been a major source of bugs in Node.js applications. Historically, Node just printed a warning and continued, which meant errors could silently corrupt application state. Since Node 15+, unhandled rejections crash the process by default (like uncaught exceptions), which is much safer. The event is 'unhandledRejection' on the process object. Best practices include: always having a .catch() at the end of every promise chain, using async/await with try/catch instead of raw promises, setting up a global handler as a safety net (not as primary error handling), and in production using a process manager like PM2 that restarts on crashes. A subtle case is when you create a promise but don't await it — fire-and-forget async operations. These need explicit .catch() because there's no caller to propagate the error to. Another pattern is using Promise.allSettled instead of Promise.all when you don't want one failure to kill the entire operation.
</think>

```javascript
// === Node.js unhandled rejection behavior ===

// Node < 15: Warning only (dangerous — errors silently ignored)
// Node >= 15: Crashes process (safe default, like uncaught exceptions)
// Node >= 16.17: --unhandled-rejections=throw is default

// Global safety net — catches rejections that slip through
process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason, promise) => {
  console.error('Unhandled Rejection at:', promise);
  console.error('Reason:', reason);
  // In production: log to monitoring service, then crash
  // Do NOT swallow — crash and let process manager restart
  process.exit(1);
});

// === Common sources of unhandled rejections ===

// 1. Fire-and-forget async calls
async function saveMetrics(data) {
  await db.insert('metrics', data); // Can throw
}

function handleRequest(req, res) {
  res.json({ ok: true });
  saveMetrics(req.body); // ❌ No await, no .catch() — unhandled if it throws!
}

// FIX: Explicit .catch() for fire-and-forget
function handleRequestFixed(req, res) {
  res.json({ ok: true });
  saveMetrics(req.body).catch(err => {
    console.error('Metrics save failed (non-critical):', err.message);
  });
}

// 2. Missing .catch() on promise chains
fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(processData);
  // ❌ No .catch() — if any step throws, unhandled rejection

// FIX:
fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(processData)
  .catch(err => console.error('Pipeline failed:', err));

// 3. Async event handlers
const EventEmitter = require('events');
const emitter = new EventEmitter();

// ❌ Async listener — thrown error becomes unhandled rejection
emitter.on('data', async (payload) => {
  await riskyOperation(payload); // Throws → unhandled!
});

// FIX: Wrap async event handlers
function safeAsync(fn) {
  return (...args) => {
    fn(...args).catch(err => {
      console.error('Async handler error:', err);
    });
  };
}

emitter.on('data', safeAsync(async (payload) => {
  await riskyOperation(payload); // Now caught
}));

// === Best practices ===

// Always use async/await with try/catch at the top level
async function main() {
  try {
    const data = await loadConfig();
    const server = await startServer(data);
    console.log('Server running on', server.address());
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Fatal:', err);
    process.exit(1);
  }
}

main();
```

instruction: Explain async iterators and the for-await-of pattern in JavaScript. When are they useful and how do you create custom async iterables?
output:
<think>
Async iterators extend the iterator protocol for asynchronous data sources. A regular iterator's next() returns {value, done}, while an async iterator's next() returns a Promise<{value, done}>. The for-await-of loop consumes async iterables, awaiting each value before proceeding. This is perfect for streaming data: reading files line by line, consuming paginated APIs, processing database cursors, or handling WebSocket messages. The key insight is that async iterators handle backpressure naturally — the consumer pulls values one at a time, waiting for each before requesting the next. This prevents memory overflow from fast producers. You create async iterables by implementing Symbol.asyncIterator, or more commonly by using async generator functions (async function*). Async generators are the most ergonomic way to produce async sequences — you can use await inside them and yield values as they become available. Node.js Readable streams implement the async iterable protocol, so you can for-await-of directly on them.
</think>

```javascript
// === Async generator: the most common way to create async iterables ===

async function* paginate(baseUrl, pageSize = 10) {
  let page = 1;
  let hasMore = true;

  while (hasMore) {
    const response = await fetch(`${baseUrl}?page=${page}&size=${pageSize}`);
    const data = await response.json();

    for (const item of data.items) {
      yield item; // Yield one item at a time — consumer controls pace
    }

    hasMore = data.items.length === pageSize;
    page++;
  }
}

// Consumer: for-await-of handles the async waiting
async function processAllUsers() {
  for await (const user of paginate('/api/users', 50)) {
    await processUser(user); // Natural backpressure — one at a time
  }
}

// === Custom async iterable class ===

class EventStream {
  constructor(eventSource) {
    this.source = eventSource;
  }

  // Implement Symbol.asyncIterator to make it for-await-of compatible
  [Symbol.asyncIterator]() {
    const queue = [];
    let resolve = null;
    let done = false;

    this.source.on('data', (event) => {
      if (resolve) {
        resolve({ value: event, done: false });
        resolve = null;
      } else {
        queue.push(event);
      }
    });

    this.source.on('end', () => {
      done = true;
      if (resolve) resolve({ value: undefined, done: true });
    });

    return {
      next() {
        if (queue.length > 0) {
          return Promise.resolve({ value: queue.shift(), done: false });
        }
        if (done) {
          return Promise.resolve({ value: undefined, done: true });
        }
        return new Promise(r => { resolve = r; });
      },
      return() {
        done = true;
        return Promise.resolve({ value: undefined, done: true });
      }
    };
  }
}

// === Node.js streams are async iterable ===
const fs = require('fs');
const readline = require('readline');

async function processLargeFile(path) {
  const rl = readline.createInterface({
    input: fs.createReadStream(path),
    crlfDelay: Infinity
  });

  let lineNum = 0;
  for await (const line of rl) {
    lineNum++;
    if (line.startsWith('ERROR')) {
      console.log(`Line ${lineNum}: ${line}`);
    }
  }
  // Memory-safe: only one line in memory at a time
}

// === Utility: merge multiple async iterables ===
async function* merge(...iterables) {
  const iterators = iterables.map(it => it[Symbol.asyncIterator]());
  const pending = new Map();

  for (const [i, iter] of iterators.entries()) {
    pending.set(i, iter.next().then(result => ({ i, result })));
  }

  while (pending.size > 0) {
    const { i, result } = await Promise.race(pending.values());
    if (result.done) {
      pending.delete(i);
    } else {
      yield result.value;
      pending.set(i, iterators[i].next().then(result => ({ i, result })));
    }
  }
}
```
